name: Dandelion
short_description: Dandelion is a traditional botanical used in teas and supplements for digestive, liver, urinary, and general wellness education.
overview_subtitle: A practical look at dandelion root, leaf, and whole-plant use in modern supplement labels.
overview_content:
Dandelion is the common name for Taraxacum officinale, a widely recognized flowering plant used in foods, teas, traditional herbal preparations, and dietary supplements. Although many people think of dandelion as a common yard plant, its roots and leaves have a long history of use in botanical wellness traditions. In supplement education, dandelion is usually discussed for digestive support, urinary wellness, liver-support formulas, and general detox-positioned products. These uses should be interpreted carefully because dandelion is not a medical detox treatment and should not replace diagnosis or prescribed care.

Different parts of the dandelion plant are used differently. Dandelion leaf is often associated with urinary and fluid-balance conversations because it may have mild diuretic properties. Dandelion root is more commonly used in digestive and liver-support blends, especially teas, powders, capsules, and extracts. A product may list dandelion root, dandelion leaf, whole herb, or dandelion extract. Those forms are not interchangeable, so the plant part matters when comparing labels.

Dandelion contains naturally occurring compounds such as bitter constituents, phenolic acids, flavonoids, inulin-type fiber, and minerals. Bitter botanicals are often discussed for their role in digestive routines because bitter flavors can be part of traditional pre-meal herbal practices. However, supplement labels should avoid promising liver cleansing, weight loss, or disease treatment. A responsible dandelion profile focuses on plant part, dose, safety cautions, and whether the product explains its intended use clearly.

Safety context is important. Memorial Sloan Kettering notes potential interactions with diuretics and hypoglycemic medications, and people with ragweed-family allergies may be sensitive to dandelion. People with gallbladder disease, bile duct obstruction, kidney disease, diabetes, medication use, pregnancy, or nursing should seek professional guidance before using dandelion supplements. Because dandelion leaf can be positioned around fluid balance, it is especially important not to combine it casually with prescription diuretics.

Suppriva presents dandelion as an educational ingredient profile for label comparison, not as a recommendation for self-treatment. The most useful questions are: which part of the plant is used, is it root or leaf, is the product a tea or concentrated extract, what dose is listed, and what safety cautions apply to the user? That approach helps readers compare dandelion products without overextending the evidence.

Dandelion also appears in many wellness formulas that use broad language such as cleanse, detox, bloat support, or liver support. Those phrases can be confusing because they often refer to general structure-function positioning rather than proven clinical outcomes. Readers should look for specific ingredient facts instead of relying on marketing language. If a product does not identify whether it uses root, leaf, or extract, it is harder to evaluate responsibly.

Diet and lifestyle context matters too. Digestive comfort, fluid balance, and metabolic wellness are influenced by hydration, sodium intake, fiber, medication use, medical conditions, and overall eating pattern. Dandelion may be one botanical in a wellness routine, but persistent swelling, abdominal pain, urinary changes, jaundice, or unexplained digestive symptoms should be evaluated by a healthcare professional rather than managed with supplements alone.

For content and dashboard use, dandelion should be written with plant-part precision. A short product card may simply say dandelion, but a full ingredient page should explain whether the user is likely seeing root, leaf, whole herb, tea, or extract. That clarity keeps the page useful for both supplement shoppers and internal linking because it prevents one broad botanical name from becoming a vague catch-all.
interesting_fact: Dandelion leaves, roots, and flowers are all used in different traditions, but supplement labels should specify the plant part because root and leaf are not the same ingredient context.
how_it_works_subtitle: Dandelion is reviewed through bitter botanical, digestive, urinary, and fluid-balance education.
how_it_works_highlight_description: Dandelion root is usually discussed for digestive and liver-support formulas, while dandelion leaf is more often discussed for urinary and fluid-balance routines.
how_it_works_content:
Dandelion works as a botanical ingredient rather than a single isolated nutrient. The leaf, root, and whole plant contain different profiles of bitter compounds, flavonoids, phenolic acids, and fiber-like constituents. This is why the plant part should be identified clearly on the label.

Dandelion leaf is often discussed for mild diuretic activity and fluid-balance education. Dandelion root is commonly positioned in digestive bitters, liver-support teas, and botanical blends. These traditional uses do not mean dandelion treats kidney, liver, digestive, or metabolic disease.

A responsible review focuses on whether the product uses tea, powder, tincture, capsule, or extract; whether the plant part is clear; and whether safety cautions are appropriate. People using diuretics, diabetes medication, lithium, or medications affected by fluid balance should involve a healthcare professional.

dosage_subtitle: Serving size depends on whether the product uses root, leaf, tea, powder, tincture, or extract.
typical_dose: Common labels vary widely, with teas used by cup and capsules often providing several hundred milligrams per serving.
dosage: Common labels vary widely, with teas used by cup and capsules often providing several hundred milligrams per serving.
dosage_content:
Dandelion dosage depends heavily on form. Herbal teas may use dried root or leaf by the cup, while capsules may provide powdered herb or extract in several-hundred-milligram servings. Tinctures and liquid extracts use different measurement systems, so the product label should be followed closely.

Because dandelion may affect fluid balance and may interact with certain medications, more is not automatically better. People using diuretics, diabetes medication, lithium, blood pressure medication, or those with kidney or gallbladder concerns should ask a healthcare professional before use.
safety_subtitle: Dandelion is generally tolerated by many adults, but allergies, diuretic effects, glucose monitoring, and gallbladder concerns matter.

side_effects_json.title: Digestive Upset
side_effects_json.description: Dandelion may cause stomach discomfort, heartburn, gas, or loose stools in sensitive users.

side_effects_json.title: Allergy Risk
side_effects_json.description: People allergic to ragweed, daisies, chrysanthemums, or related plants may react to dandelion.

side_effects_json.title: Fluid-Balance Caution
side_effects_json.description: Dandelion leaf may have diuretic activity and should be reviewed with relevant medications.

side_effects_json.title: Gallbladder Review
side_effects_json.description: People with gallbladder disease or bile duct obstruction should seek clinician guidance.

drug_interactions_json: Diuretic medications may have additive effects with dandelion leaf or fluid-balance formulas.
Diabetes medications may require monitoring if dandelion is used in glucose-related routines.
Lithium should be reviewed because changes in fluid balance may affect lithium handling.
Blood pressure medications and kidney-related prescriptions should be discussed with a healthcare professional.
who_should_avoid_json: People with ragweed-family allergies unless cleared by a clinician.
People using prescription diuretics without professional guidance.
People with gallbladder disease, bile duct obstruction, kidney disease, or significant fluid-balance concerns.
Pregnant or nursing individuals unless advised by a qualified healthcare professional.
Anyone using dandelion as a replacement for liver, kidney, digestive, or urinary medical care.
food_sources_subtitle: Dandelion can be used as a food and herbal preparation, though supplements are more concentrated.
food_sources_content: Dandelion leaves are sometimes eaten as bitter greens, while roasted dandelion root is used in herbal beverages. Flowers may also appear in traditional food preparations. Supplement products can be stronger or more concentrated than culinary use, so labels should be reviewed separately.

faq_subtitle: Common questions about Dandelion, supplement use, dosage, safety, and label comparison.

faq_json.question: What is dandelion?
faq_json.answer: Dandelion is Taraxacum officinale, a botanical used in foods, teas, and supplements for digestive, urinary, and general wellness education.

faq_json.question: Is dandelion root the same as dandelion leaf?
faq_json.answer: No. Root and leaf are different plant parts and are often used in different supplement contexts.

faq_json.question: What is dandelion commonly used for?
faq_json.answer: It is commonly discussed in digestive bitters, herbal teas, fluid-balance formulas, and liver-support products.

faq_json.question: Can dandelion detox the liver?
faq_json.answer: No supplement should be viewed as a medical detox treatment. Dandelion may appear in liver-support formulas, but it does not replace liver care.

faq_json.question: Can dandelion interact with diuretics?
faq_json.answer: Yes. Dandelion leaf may have diuretic activity, so prescription diuretics should be reviewed with a clinician.

faq_json.question: Can people with ragweed allergies use dandelion?
faq_json.answer: They should be cautious because dandelion is in the Asteraceae family and may trigger reactions in sensitive people.

faq_json.question: What is a typical dandelion dose?
faq_json.answer: It depends on whether the product is tea, powder, capsule, tincture, root, leaf, or extract. Follow the label.

faq_json.question: Is dandelion safe during pregnancy?
faq_json.answer: Pregnant or nursing individuals should ask a qualified healthcare professional before using dandelion supplements.

faq_json.question: What should I look for on a dandelion label?
faq_json.answer: Look for plant part, form, serving size, extract details, allergen cautions, and medication warnings.

faq_json.question: Can dandelion replace medical care?
faq_json.answer: No. Dandelion is educationally reviewed as a supplement ingredient and should not replace diagnosis or treatment.

research_subtitle: A balanced interpretation of available supplement research.
research_content:
Dandelion research includes plant chemistry, diuretic activity, digestive use, and preclinical liver-related studies. Human evidence is limited and should not be overstated.

The most responsible interpretation is plant-part-specific education combined with medication and allergy caution.

research_json.title: Form Matters
research_json.description: Research findings may not apply equally to every extract, dose, or delivery format.

research_json.title: Population Matters
research_json.description: Outcomes can vary by baseline status, diet, medication use, health conditions, and study design.

references_subtitle: References should be reviewed by ingredient form, dose, and health context.
